title: Charmed Life - Chapter 7 (Fragment 2)
text:
“I want Cat,” she said.

“He’s busy,” said Roger.

“That doesn’t matter,” said Gwendolen. “I need him.”

Julia stretched out a knitting needle towards Gwendolen and wrote a little cross in the air with it. The cross floated, glowing, for a second. “Out,” said Julia. “Go away.” Gwendolen backed away from the cross and shut the door again. It was as if she could not help herself. The expression on her face was very annoyed indeed. Julia smiled placidly and pointed her knitting needle towards Cat’s soldiers. “Carry on,” she said. “I’ve filled their hearts with courage.”

When the dressing gong sounded, Cat went to find out what Gwendolen had wanted him for. Gwendolen was very busy reading a fat, new-looking book and could not spare him any attention at first. Cat tipped his head sideways and read the title of the book. Otherworld Studies, Series III. While he was doing it, Gwendolen began to laugh. “Oh, I see how it works now!” she exclaimed. “It’s even better than I thought! I know what to do now!” Then she lowered the book and asked Cat what he thought he was doing.

“Why did you need me?” said Cat. “Where did you get that book?”

“From the Castle library,” said Gwendolen. “And I don’t need you now. I was going to explain to you about Mr. Nostrum’s plans, and I might even have told you about mine, but I changed my mind when you just sat there and let that fat prig Julia send me away.”

“I didn’t know Mr. Nostrum had any plans,” Cat said. “The dressing gong’s gone.”

“Of course he has plans—and I heard it—why do you think I wrote to Chrestomanci?” said Gwendolen. “But it’s no good trying to wheedle me. I’m not going to tell you and you’re going to be sorry. And piggy-priggy Julia is going to be sorrier even sooner!”

Gwendolen revenged herself on Julia at the start of dinner. A footman was just passing a bowl of soup over Julia’s shoulder when the skirt of Julia’s dress turned to snakes. Julia jumped up with a shriek. Soup poured over the snakes and flew far and wide, and the footman yelled, “Lord have mercy on us!” among the sounds of the smashing soup bowl.

Then there was dead silence, except for the hissing of snakes. There were twenty of them, hanging by their tails from Julia’s waistband, writhing and striking. Everyone froze, with their heads stiffly turned Julia’s way. Julia stood like a statue, with her arms up out of reach of the snakes. She swallowed and said the words of a spell.

Nobody blamed her. Mr. Saunders said, “Good girl!”

Under the spell, the snakes stiffened and fanned out, so that they were standing like a ballet skirt above Julia’s petticoats. Everyone could see where Julia had torn a flounce of a petticoat building the tree house and mended it in a hurry with red darning wool.

“Have you been bitten?” said Chrestomanci.

“No,” said Julia. “The soup muddled them. If you don’t mind, I’ll go and change this dress now.”

She left the room, walking very slowly and carefully, and Millie went with her. While the footmen, all rather green in the face, were clearing up the spilled soup, Chrestomanci said, “Spitefulness is one thing I won’t have at the dinner table. Gwendolen, oblige me by going to the playroom. Your food will be brought to you there.”

vocabulary.definition: Having a great deal to do; actively engaged in activity.
vocabulary.example: “He’s busy,” said Roger.

vocabulary.definition: Words that are often used together with a specific meaning or typical combination.
vocabulary.example: Avoid very basic words unless contextually significant.

vocabulary.definition: The ability to do something that frightens one; bravery.
vocabulary.example: Julia smiled placidly and pointed her knitting needle towards Cat’s soldiers. “Carry on,” she said. “I’ve filled their hearts with courage.”

vocabulary.definition: A mark, object, or figure formed by two intersecting lines or bars.
vocabulary.example: Julia stretched out a knitting needle towards Gwendolen and wrote a little cross in the air with it.

vocabulary.definition: The act of repairing fabric by sewing.
vocabulary.example: …and mended it in a hurry with red darning wool.

vocabulary.definition: To cry out suddenly, especially in surprise, anger, or pain.
vocabulary.example: Then she lowered the book and asked Cat what he thought he was doing.

vocabulary.definition: A strip of fabric gathered or pleated on one edge and attached to a garment as a trimming or decoration.
vocabulary.example: Everyone could see where Julia had torn a flounce of a petticoat building the tree house and mended it in a hurry.

vocabulary.definition: To move or flap quickly and lightly.
vocabulary.example: The cross floated, glowing, for a second.

vocabulary.definition: A male servant who waits at table, opens doors, etc., especially in a large house.
vocabulary.example: A footman was just passing a bowl of soup over Julia’s shoulder when the skirt of Julia’s dress turned to snakes.

vocabulary.definition: Unable to move or act due to surprise or fear.
vocabulary.example: Everyone froze, with their heads stiffly turned Julia’s way.

vocabulary.definition: A sharp sibilant sound.
vocabulary.example: Then there was dead silence, except for the hissing of snakes.

vocabulary.definition: A slender pointed rod used to knit yarn into fabric.
vocabulary.example: Julia stretched out a knitting needle towards Gwendolen and wrote a little cross in the air.

vocabulary.definition: Make (someone) legally or morally bound to do something; to compel or ask politely.
vocabulary.example: Chrestomanci said, “Gwendolen, oblige me by going to the playroom.”

vocabulary.definition: An underskirt worn by women or girls.
vocabulary.example: Under the spell, the snakes stiffened and fanned out, so that they were standing like a ballet skirt above Julia’s petticoats.

vocabulary.definition: In a calm and peaceful manner, without disturbance or agitation.
vocabulary.example: Julia smiled placidly and pointed her knitting needle towards Cat’s soldiers.

vocabulary.definition: To inflict harm or punishment in return for (an injury or wrong done to oneself or another).
vocabulary.example: Gwendolen revenged herself on Julia at the start of dinner.

vocabulary.definition: A high-pitched, piercing cry or sound expressing terror, pain, or excitement.
vocabulary.example: Julia jumped up with a shriek.

vocabulary.definition: Formed one’s lips into a pleased, kind, or amused expression.
vocabulary.example: Julia smiled placidly and pointed her knitting needle towards Cat’s soldiers.

vocabulary.definition: A form of words used as a magical charm or incantation.
vocabulary.example: She swallowed and said the words of a spell.

vocabulary.definition: Caused or allowed (liquid) to flow over the edge of its container.
vocabulary.example: While the footmen… were clearing up the spilled soup.

vocabulary.definition: The quality of wanting to annoy, hurt, or upset someone deliberately.
vocabulary.example: Chrestomanci said, “Spitefulness is one thing I won’t have at the dinner table.”

vocabulary.definition: A carved or cast figure of a person or animal, especially one that is life-size or larger.
vocabulary.example: Julia stood like a statue, with her arms up out of reach of the snakes.

vocabulary.definition: To pull or rip something apart or to pieces.
vocabulary.example: Everyone could see where Julia had torn a flounce of a petticoat.

vocabulary.definition: To persuade someone to do something or give one something by using flattery or coaxing.
vocabulary.example: “But it’s no good trying to wheedle me. I’m not going to tell you and you’re going to be sorry.”

vocabulary.definition: Making twisting, squirming movements or contortions of the body.
vocabulary.example: There were twenty of them, hanging by their tails from Julia’s waistband, writhing and striking.
